name: Pine Forests of Guadarrama Trail
name_original: Alto de la Jarosa — circular desde Guadarrama
description: This difficult 12.4-mile touring cycling route in Sierra de Guadarrama National Park offers panoramic views and varied, technical terrain.
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Guadarrama traverse a diverse landscape of mountainous terrain, extensive pine forests, and distinctive granite formations. The region features significant peaks, such as Peñalara, and valleys like La Fuenfría, offering varied elevation profiles. Rivers such as the Lozoya and Manzanares flow through the valleys, adding to the scenic beauty. This area provides a range of routes suitable for different cycling preferences and skill levels. Best touring cycling routes around Guadarrama The most popular touring cycling route is Pine Forests of Guadarrama Trail, a 12.4 miles (19.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 34 minutes to complete. This route offers a challenging ride through dense woodlands. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Valmayor Reservoir – Valmayor Reservoir loop from Guadarrama, a moderate 24.0 miles (38.7 km) path. This route circles the reservoir, providing views of the water and surrounding natural areas. Local touring cyclists also love the La Jarosa Reservoir – La Jarosa Reservoir loop from Guadarrama, a 15.1 miles (24.3 km) trail leading through varied terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 15 minutes. Touring cycling around Guadarrama is defined by mountainous terrain, pine forests, and granite formations. The network offers options for both moderate paths and challenging ascents. The routes in Guadarrama are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.75 stars from over 5 reviews. More than 250 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Guadarrama's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available in Guadarrama?
A: Guadarrama offers a diverse network of over 18 touring cycling routes, catering to various skill levels. You'll find everything from moderate paths to challenging ascents through its mountainous terrain and pine forests.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Guadarrama?
A: The optimal time for touring cycling in Guadarrama is typically from late April to mid-November. This period offers mild summers and colorful autumns, providing pleasant conditions for exploring the region's natural beauty.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly touring cycling routes in Guadarrama?
A: Yes, Guadarrama has options suitable for beginners. Out of the available routes, 5 are classified as easy. While specific easy routes aren't detailed here, you can find routes with less elevation gain and smoother terrain to start your cycling adventure.

Q: Can I find circular touring cycling routes in Guadarrama?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in Guadarrama are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. A popular moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33953275" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valmayor Reservoir – Valmayor Reservoir loop from Guadarrama&lt;/a&gt;, which offers scenic views around the water. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14915041" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Jarosa Reservoir – La Jarosa Reservoir loop from Guadarrama&lt;/a&gt;, a more challenging 24.3 km ride through varied terrain.

Q: What kind of natural features and landmarks can I expect to see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Guadarrama's routes are rich in natural and historical sights. You can cycle past significant peaks like Peñalara, through vast pine forests, and alongside rivers such as the Lozoya and Manzanares. Keep an eye out for unique granite formations, especially in areas like La Pedriza. Historic sites like the Royal Monastery of El Escorial and the Roman Road of Puerto de la Fuenfría are also within the region. For specific points of interest, consider exploring highlights such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2505177" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mount Abantos&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/121253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seven Peaks&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any challenging touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, Guadarrama is well-known for its challenging terrain, including mountain passes that have featured in the Vuelta a España. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1189936023/pine-forests-of-guadarrama-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pine Forests of Guadarrama Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a 19.9 km ride with significant elevation, are classified as difficult and will test experienced cyclists.

Q: Are there any family-friendly touring cycling routes in Guadarrama?
A: While the region is known for its mountainous terrain, there are easier sections and paths suitable for families. Look for routes classified as 'easy' within the guide, which typically feature less elevation and smoother surfaces. The extensive network includes options for various skill levels, so you can find a path that suits your family's abilities.

Q: What do other touring cyclists say about the routes in Guadarrama?
A: The touring cycling routes in Guadarrama are highly regarded by the komoot community, boasting an impressive average rating of 4.75 stars from over 7 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from dense pine forests to scenic reservoir views, and the variety of routes available for different fitness levels.

Q: Where can I find places to eat or rest near the cycling routes?
A: The charming villages scattered throughout the Guadarrama landscape, such as Cercedilla, Manzanares el Real, and Navacerrada, serve as excellent starting points and offer local gastronomy. You'll find cafes, pubs, and restaurants in these areas to refuel and relax after your ride.

Q: Is public transport available to access the cycling routes in Guadarrama?
A: Guadarrama is conveniently located less than an hour from Madrid, making it accessible by public transport. Many villages that serve as route starting points, like Cercedilla, are connected by regional train or bus services, allowing cyclists to reach the area without a car. It's advisable to check specific routes and schedules for the most up-to-date information.

Q: Are there specific parking facilities for cyclists in Guadarrama?
A: Yes, many of the villages and popular trailheads in Guadarrama offer designated parking areas for visitors. Villages like Manzanares el Real and Cercedilla, which are common starting points for cycling routes, typically have parking available. Always check local signage for any restrictions or fees.

Q: Can I cycle near historical sites or cultural attractions?
A: Yes, Guadarrama blends natural beauty with rich history. You can cycle routes that pass near or offer detours to historical sites such as the Royal Monastery of El Escorial or the well-preserved Roman Road of Puerto de la Fuenfría. The medieval &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6556720" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castle of Manzanares el Real&lt;/a&gt; is another notable landmark worth exploring.

Q: What is the terrain like on Guadarrama's touring cycling routes?
A: The terrain in Guadarrama is highly varied, characterized by dramatic mountainous landscapes, extensive pine forests, and distinctive granite formations. You'll encounter everything from paved roads with gentle curves to challenging ascents on technical, sometimes unpaved, segments. Rivers like the Lozoya and Manzanares also add to the scenic diversity.

The reservoir ends up being like a lake that forms a beautiful ensemble with the silhouette of the Sierra de Guadarrama in the background.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route suitable for beginners or standard touring bicycles?
faqs.answer: No, this route is officially categorized as 'difficult' and requires 'Very good fitness'. While it features mostly paved surfaces, there are significant unpaved, technical, and steep sections. It's more suited for experienced mountain bikers or cyclists with robust touring bikes capable of handling varied and challenging off-road conditions, rather than a leisurely ride on a standard touring bicycle.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this circular touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: The terrain is varied and challenging. You can expect a mix of paved surfaces, but also unpaved segments, 'duras pendientes' (hard slopes), and 'trialeras entre pinos y helechos' (technical singletracks among pines and ferns). Some parts may include 'mucha piedra, escalones, tramos pedroleros' (lots of stones, steps, stony sections) and 'losas de roca y peraltes naturales' (rock slabs and natural berms). Be prepared for sections where you might need to push your bike.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access this circular route from Guadarrama?
faqs.answer: The route is circular from Guadarrama, implying a start and end point within the town. While specific parking locations aren't detailed, Guadarrama offers various public parking options. It's advisable to look for parking near the assumed starting point of the loop within the town.

faqs.question: What are the main scenic points or highlights along the way?
faqs.answer: The route offers 'Espectaculares panorámicas' (spectacular panoramic views) throughout. Key highlights include View of Madrid from Alto de La Jarosa and impressive views of the La Jarosa Reservoir. You'll also cycle through a nice pine forest, which provides shade and a cool environment in summer, and enjoy views of the Cuelgamuros valley.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the 'nice pine forest which makes it cool in summer' and the challenging nature of the route, spring and autumn are generally ideal. These seasons offer pleasant temperatures and stable weather conditions, avoiding the intense heat of summer and potential snow or ice in winter, which could make the technical sections even more difficult.

faqs.question: Are there any specific regulations or permits required for cycling in Sierra de Guadarrama National Park?
faqs.answer: Yes, as this route is within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, specific regulations apply to protect its natural environment. While general access for cycling is permitted on designated routes, it's crucial to stay on marked trails, respect wildlife, and adhere to any specific rules regarding group sizes or protected areas. There are typically no entrance fees for the park itself, but always check the official park website for the latest regulations on cycling and any potential permit requirements for certain activities or zones.

faqs.question: Is this touring bicycle route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park generally allows dogs, they must be kept on a leash at all times to protect wildlife and ensure the safety of other visitors. Given the technical and challenging nature of this specific cycling route, it might not be the most comfortable or safest option for dogs, especially on the 'pista y camino roto' (track and broken path) or 'tramos pedroleros' (stony sections).

faqs.question: What equipment or gear should I bring for this challenging touring bicycle route?
faqs.answer: Given the 'difficult' rating and varied terrain, it's essential to bring a robust touring bicycle or mountain bike suitable for technical sections. Recommended gear includes a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, plenty of water and snacks, a repair kit (including spare tubes and tools), a first-aid kit, and a fully charged phone with the route downloaded. Due to potential unpaved and stony sections, sturdy tires are highly advisable.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other trails in the area?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route overlaps with several other trails in the Sierra de Guadarrama area. Notably, it covers significant portions of the 'Pinares de Guadarrama' (85%) and 'Bosque plateado' (32%) trails. Smaller sections also coincide with 'Por las trincheras' (17%), 'La Camorza - La Jarosa' (10%), and 'En busca del agua' (9%), among others.

faqs.question: Given its difficulty, are there any specific sections of the route I should be prepared for?
faqs.answer: Yes, be prepared for 'duras pendientes' (hard slopes) and technical sections described as 'trialeras entre pinos y helechos' (technical singletracks among pines and ferns). The research also mentions 'mucha piedra, escalones, tramos pedroleros' (lots of stones, steps, stony sections) and 'losas de roca y peraltes naturales' (rock slabs and natural berms). While specific named sections for this exact route aren't detailed, the general character of the La Jarosa area suggests challenging climbs and descents, particularly on unpaved segments.
